items.q: What is the value of x^0 (for x not equal to zero)?
items.correct: 1 — any non-zero number to the power of zero equals 1.
items.misconception: x^0 equals 0 because raising something to the zero gives nothing.
items.random_wrong: x^0 equals x because the zero has no effect.
items.why: Students reason 'zero of anything is zero', applying additive intuition to exponents — they don't see x^0 as the result of x^1 / x^1 = 1.

items.q: What is the relationship between the angle at the centre and the angle at the circumference subtended by the same arc?
items.correct: The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference.
items.misconception: The angles are equal because they are subtended by the same arc.
items.random_wrong: The angle at the centre is half the angle at the circumference.
items.why: Students intuitively expect 'same arc = same angle' and don't internalise that the central angle is double the inscribed angle.

items.correct: 7 - x — the minus sign outside the bracket changes the sign of every term inside (3 - x + 4).
items.misconception: 3 - x - 4 = -1 - x, by only applying the minus to the first term inside.
items.why: Students treat the leading minus as if it only affects the first term, forgetting that subtracting a bracket changes every sign inside.

items.q: Which proof technique is best for disproving 'all primes are odd'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: counterexample. “induction” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Induction is most suited for statements about:
items.why: Correct answer: positive integers. “real numbers in general” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Smallest even prime (used as counterexample to 'all primes are odd').
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
